SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper reports empirical results (e.g., Table 3, concordance rates 71–95% and best/worst identical-rate 78%/43%) and describes the use of Random Forests with semantic and surface features, but experimental methodology has weaknesses. They rely on internal RF OOB estimates and state 'RFs do not need cross validation' yet also admit near-100% concordance when comparing final model to training data due to too many predictors for the sample size, indicating overfitting risk. Confusion matrices were omitted and no statistical tests or held-out validation are presented, leaving some claims insufficiently supported.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The approach largely applies established techniques (Random Forests, cosine semantic similarity, keyword matching) to Japanese short-answer scoring; the paper repeatedly cites prior systems (Jess, c-rater) and does not introduce a novel algorithmic contribution. The paper's novelty lies mainly in an application/engineering effort for a particular national test context rather than new methods or theoretical advances.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized into sections (methods, evaluation, conclusion) and presents tables/figures (Table 3, Figure 4) that convey results, but the text contains repetitions, formatting issues and some unclear phrasing (several duplicated paragraphs and sentences). Important details are omitted or referred to as 'space limitations' (e.g., omitted confusion matrices), which reduces clarity and makes reproduction harder.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The work includes real test items, multiple features, and variable-importance analysis (Figure 4) and reports per-item probabilities within one point, but the experimental scope is small (8 items, sample sizes 70–120) and lacks ablation/sensitivity studies. The paper acknowledges limitations (imbalanced scores, many zeros) but does not provide thorough analyses (no held-out test sets, no detailed error analysis or full confusion matrices).

IMPACT.justification: Automating short-answer scoring for national entrance exams is an important applied problem and the system could be useful in practice (paper notes potential deployment for Japanese university entrance tests). However, the contribution is incremental and the limited experimental validation reduces confidence that the results will strongly change research or practice broadly.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (automated short-answer scoring, feature engineering, semantic similarity) falls within NLP/ML conference scope and will interest the venue's audience, but the paper's limited methodological novelty and modest empirical evaluation make it borderline for a top-tier ML/NLP conference. The methods and evaluation are standard for applied NLP work but lack rigorous benchmarks expected at top venues.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Comparisons are only between the RF predictions and human ratings for the provided items; there are no experimental baselines (e.g., SVM, neural models, or existing automated scorers) run on the same data. The paper cites prior systems (Jess, c-rater) in related work but does not provide empirical head-to-head or ablation comparisons to these competing approaches.
